content: For those here who make presentations by hand drawing them, what specific techniques do you use? As a rough cut, I used my whiteboard and photographed with phone. I like the palpable expressivity and erasability of the whiteboard, and the way the different colored pens look. On the other hand, I ran into lighting issues using the camera. It tended to look dim, with a tan background (it was evening). But when I added extra lights, there was a brightness gradient if the bulb was off to the side. And if the bulb was too centered, there was a hotspot of brightness created by the reflectivity of the whiteboard. For a plan B, I may give up on the whiteboard and use plain white paper, with a bright overhead lamp, and some kind of apparatus for holding the iphone in place. Do people use schemes with drawing software / tablets which feel truly natural?
I think most of us use electronic methods of drawing. @Tai-Danae Bradley draws by hand really well - on a tablet, though.

(Thanks @John Baez!) Yes, I do a lot of hand-drawn math, @David Tanzer. You can see a sample video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wiadG3ywJIs. The video is not a seminar presentation, but I made it in a note-taking app (called Notability) with an Apple Pencil and just recorded the iPad screen. I have also given a Zoom talk while writing on a tablet, and I plan to do it again soon. It feels very natural to me.
@Tai-Danae Bradley Nice, thanks for the pointers! The content is very interesting too. Time to dust off the iPad...

Tai-Danae Bradley said:

(Thanks John Baez!) Yes, I do a lot of hand-drawn math, David Tanzer. You can see a sample video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wiadG3ywJIs.

After spending the past month interacting with category theorists on this site, it was a very grounding experience to hear the question "what does any of this have to do with category theory?" two thirds of the way through a presentation where compositionality was the first idea presented :laughing: A good reminder that CT is still far enough from mainstream that mathematicians won't jump to it when seeing dots and arrows in the same way that they will jump to linear algebra when they see matrices, say.
